1.exposition: Chapter four showed the throne and its ceaseless worship; now something rests in the hand of the One who sits there. On the open right palm lies a scroll written within and on the back, sealed with seven seals — God's full and secret purpose for history, closed to every eye. A strong angel cries through the universe, “Who is worthy to open the scroll?” and the silence is total: no one in heaven or on earth or under the earth can so much as look on it. John weeps much — the future seems sealed, the martyrs unanswered, evil unchecked — until an elder stops his tears: the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered. Then comes the great reversal. John turns to see the Lion and beholds instead a Lamb standing, as though slain, who takes the scroll from the right hand of God. The conqueror is the crucified.
1.original_language: The scroll is a biblion — a papyrus roll, here written esōthen kai opisthen (“within and on the back”), an opisthograph so crammed that the writing overflows to the reverse; it is katesphragismenon, “sealed down” fast with seven seals. The angel's question is not who is able but who is axios — grammatical-historical Ellicott insists the word means moral worthiness, fitness and right, not mere strength. The elder's answer turns on enikēsen, “he conquered” (v.5) — placed first for emphasis, the great historical fact of Christ's victory. And the announced Lion appears as an arnion (“lamb,” the book's own tender word for Christ) hestēkos hōs esphagmenon — “standing, as though slaughtered”: the perfect participles hold death and life together at once, slain yet upright, the wounds permanent, the life restored.
1.historical_context: A sealed scroll in this world could be a will, a deed, or a book of destiny; a Roman testament was closed with the seven seals of seven witnesses and opened only by the rightful heir — a resonance the imagery invites. The double-sided writing recalls the roll Ezekiel saw, covered front and back with lament, and the tablets of the law written on both their sides: the decree is complete, nothing may be added. The titles are the crown-jewels of messianic hope — the lion's whelp of Judah from Jacob's blessing and the root and shoot of Jesse on whom the Spirit rests — royal and military expectations that the vision at once overturns.
1.christ: Here is the theological heart of the book. The one worthy to unseal God's purpose is announced with Judah's lion-strength and David's royal right, yet when John looks he sees a Lamb bearing the marks of slaughter — and both are true at once. Christ conquers not by devouring his enemies but by being devoured for them; the throne is reached by way of the cross. He is no mere victim: his seven horns are perfect power, his seven eyes the sevenfold Spirit sent into all the earth — the slain Lamb is omnipotent and omniscient, sharing the very throne of God. When he takes the scroll from the Father's hand, the authority of Matthew 28:18 is enacted in heaven: all authority in heaven and on earth is given to the Lamb, who alone may disclose and execute what God has purposed.

verses.note: John sees, on the open right hand of the Enthroned, a scroll written within and on the back, sealed with seven seals. grammatical-historical Robertson describes a papyrus roll lying on the palm (the preposition is epi), so full that the text runs onto the reverse — an opisthograph like Ezekiel's roll. What the scroll is, the witnesses divide over: the latin-fathers fathers Victorinus and Bede read it as holy Scripture, the Old Testament laid open through Christ's incarnation — “the Old Testament without, the New within”; most, with puritan Matthew Henry, take it as the sealed book of God's decrees, his providential purposes for the church and the world, “the things which must be hereafter.” On either reading it holds God's hidden will, and it is fast shut.

A strong angel proclaims with a loud voice, “Who is worthy to open the scroll and break its seals?” grammatical-historical Barnes notes that heralds were chosen for the power of their voice, and this challenge must reach the bounds of creation. But grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the key word: worthy is not “strong enough” but morally fit — the qualification is a matter of right and character, not force (cf. Romans 1:4). The question goes out as a quest through the whole universe, and it hangs unanswered.

The response is silence: no one in heaven or on earth or under the earth could open the scroll or even look into it. The three regions, grammatical-historical Robertson observes, name the entire universe (as in Philippians 2:10). grammatical-historical Clarke draws the sweep out: neither angels, nor men, nor the departed, nor any power above or below can fathom God's counsels; wesleyan-arminian Wesley adds his pointed “no, not Mary herself.” The failure is not incidental — it magnifies, by contrast, the worthiness of the One who can.

“And I wept much” — grammatical-historical Robertson notes the imperfect (eklaion poly): John kept on weeping, perhaps aloud, a flood of grief that the disclosure of God's purpose should seem lost to all. grammatical-historical Ellicott hears in the emphatic “I” no failure of faith but the ache of a heart to which God's dealings with the world are dear; wesleyan-arminian Wesley's famous word belongs here: “The Revelation was not written without tears; neither without tears will it be understood.”

An elder stays him: “Weep no more; behold, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered” so as to open the scroll. grammatical-historical Clarke traces the first title to Jacob's blessing on Judah, the royal lion of the messianic tribe; the second, grammatical-historical Barnes notes, to the root of Jesse — Christ both David's descendant and, more deeply, the root from which David himself sprang. The decisive word, Ellicott and Robertson agree, is conquered: his victory by life, death, and resurrection is the ground of his worthiness. Announced as a Lion, he will be seen as a Lamb.

John turns and beholds the paradox: in the midst of the throne, a Lamb standing, as though slain, with seven horns and seven eyes. puritan Matthew Henry frames it exactly: “He is a lion to conquer Satan, a lamb to satisfy the justice of God” — and he appears “with the marks of his sufferings upon him.” wesleyan-arminian Wesley catches the resurrection note in the very posture: “he stands — he lieth no more,” yet bears forever the tokens of his death. The seven horns are perfect power, the seven eyes “the seven Spirits of God sent out into all the earth” (cf. Zechariah 4:10) — the slain Lamb is omnipotent, omniscient, and enthroned.

“He came and took the scroll from the right hand of him who sat on the throne.” grammatical-historical Robertson lingers on the perfect tense (eilēphen) — a vivid, arresting “he has taken it,” the frozen frame of the decisive act. That the Lamb may approach the throne at all, grammatical-historical Gill notes, belongs to him as Mediator, where no mere creature could draw near; and with him alone, grammatical-historical Clarke adds, are all the counsels of God (John 1:18). wesleyan-arminian Wesley hears the promise of Psalm 2:8 — “Ask of me” — gloriously fulfilled: the Father commits to the Son the book of his purposes.

witnesses.point: The scroll is a papyrus roll on the open palm, written front and back and sealed fast; the perfect “he has taken it” freezes the decisive act of the Lamb receiving it.
“Worthy” means moral fitness, not mere strength; the accent falls on Christ's victory — he “conquered” so as to open the scroll.
The sealed book signifies holy Scripture, especially the Old Testament, given into Christ's hands and opened through his incarnation.
The Lamb “stands — he lieth no more,” bearing still the marks of death; the seven horns are full power, the seven eyes the all-seeing Spirit.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: The juxtaposition of Lion and Lamb is the interpretive key to the whole book: John hears of a conquering Lion but sees a slaughtered Lamb, and the second image controls the first — Christ's victory is won not by lion-like violence but by his sacrificial death, so every later scene of conquest must be read through the slain Lamb.

1.application: Set your grief beside John's. He wept as though the future were sealed and evil had the last word — and the answer to his tears was not an explanation but a person: a Lamb, still bearing his wounds, holding history in his pierced hands. Whatever seems shut and unanswerable to you, the scroll is not in the grip of chance or of Caesar. It is held by the One who conquered by being slain.

8.exposition: The taking of the scroll unlooses heaven's worship, and it swells outward in widening rings. First the innermost circle: the four living creatures and twenty-four elders fall before the Lamb with harps and golden bowls of incense — the very prayers of the saints — and sing a new song: “Worthy are you, for you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and tongue and people and nation, and made them a kingdom and priests who shall reign on the earth.” Then the circle widens to myriads of angels, crying with one voice, “Worthy is the Lamb who was slain” — a sevenfold ascription of power and praise. At last it reaches the outermost bound: every creature in heaven, earth, sea, and under the earth blesses “him who sits on the throne and the Lamb” together, forever. The living creatures seal it with “Amen,” and the elders fall down in silent worship.
8.original_language: The redeemed sing an ōdēn kainēn, a new song — kainos, new in kind, a praise that could not exist until the Lamb was slain. Its ground is stated in two verbs: esphagēs (“you were slaughtered”) and ēgorasas (“you purchased”), the language of the marketplace and of ransom — the Lamb bought a people at the price of his blood. They come from every phylē, glōssa, laos, and ethnos — tribe, tongue, people, and nation — and are made a basileian kai hiereis, “a kingdom and priests” (v.10). The angels' tribute in v.12 is deliberately sevenfold — power, riches, wisdom, might, honor, glory, blessing — the number of completeness, the whole compass of praise heaped upon the slain Lamb.
8.historical_context: The “new song” is an Old Testament phrase for praise raised at a fresh act of deliverance — Psalm 96:1, Psalm 98:1, Isaiah 42:10 — and here it celebrates a new exodus, a people ransomed not from Egypt but from every nation. The incense that is “the prayers of the saints” echoes the temple liturgy (Psalm 141:2). The sevenfold doxology mirrors the highest Jewish ascription, the sort of list David renders to God alone. And “a kingdom and priests” reaches back to Sinai's promise, now fulfilled across all peoples through the Lamb's blood (cf. Revelation 1:6). The very acclamation “Worthy!” belonged to the accession of an emperor — heaven renders to the Lamb what the world reserved for Caesar.
8.christ: The chapter's climax is a single, staggering fact of worship. In v.13 the whole creation blesses “him who sits on the throne and the Lamb” with one everlasting doxology — the identical worship offered to God is offered to the Lamb, on equal terms. What creation says of the Creator in chapter four (“Worthy are you”) it now says of the Redeemer, and the grounds differ by design: God is worthy as maker of all things, the Lamb worthy because he was slain. Creation and redemption, the two great works of God, meet in one object of worship — the Father on the throne and the Lamb before it. To adore the Lamb is not to worship a creature; it is to confess his deity.

verses.note: When the Lamb takes the scroll, the four living creatures and twenty-four elders fall down before him with harps and golden bowls of incense. grammatical-historical Robertson notes that those who worshiped the Enthroned in chapter four now prostrate themselves before the Lamb — the same homage given to God given to him. The bowls, the text explains, are “the prayers of the saints,” and puritan Matthew Henry finds the tenderness in it: the prayers of God's lowliest people rise in golden vessels, fragrant and accepted before the throne — not one of them lost.

They sing a new song: “Worthy are you to take the scroll... for you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and tongue and people and nation.” grammatical-historical Robertson marks its newness — a song consequent on redemption, impossible until the Lamb was slain (cf. Revelation 14:3). The worthiness the angel had sought is now owned in the Lamb, and on a new ground: not, as in chapter four, worthiness as Creator, but, grammatical-historical Barnes observes, worthiness by his violent death — “for you were slain.” His blood, grammatical-historical Clarke adds, knows no national bounds, purchasing the redeemed out of all the races of mankind.

The song rises to the dignity redemption confers: “you have made them a kingdom and priests to our God, and they shall reign on the earth.” grammatical-historical Robertson hears the ancient promise of Exodus 19:6 (and 1 Peter 2:9) fulfilled — believers made kings who share Christ's dominion and priests with access to God. wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the delicacy of the best text, in which the redeemed speak of themselves in the third person — “them... they” — out of deep self-abasement, owning the grace that raised ransomed sinners to royalty.

The worship widens: John hears the voice of many angels around the throne — ten thousand times ten thousand, and thousands of thousands. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the new, vaster outer circle taking up the song antiphonally; grammatical-historical Clarke hears the echo of Daniel's thousand thousands ministering before the Ancient of Days. wesleyan-arminian Wesley marks a striking detail of the arrangement: the redeemed elders stand nearer to God than the angels — the ransomed in the inner ring, the unfallen angels in the outer — for redemption gives a place even angels do not share.

The angels cry with a loud voice, “Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and riches and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing!” grammatical-historical Clarke unfolds the sevenfold tribute — omnipotence, beneficence, omniscience, prevailing strength, honor, splendor, and thanksgiving — the fullness of all praise. grammatical-historical Ellicott names it the sevenfold doxology of the angels, who (unlike the redeemed) say “worthy is the Lamb” in the third person; and wesleyan-arminian Wesley hears this sevenfold applause answering the seven seals of the scroll.

The circle reaches its widest bound: every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea ascribes “blessing and honor and glory and power” to “him who sits on the throne and the Lamb, forever.” grammatical-historical Ellicott calls this the chorus of the universe; wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the fourfold praise agreeing with the four regions of the world. The decisive point, the witnesses agree, is the joint object: God and the Lamb praised together, on equal terms — a plain testimony to the Lamb's deity, the creature's adoration rendered to him with the Father.

The worship is sealed where it began. The four living creatures say “Amen,” and the elders fall down and worship. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the fitting symmetry — the creatures and elders who opened heaven's praise in chapter four now close it; the song that swelled outward through angels and all creation returns to the inner circle's wordless adoration. Their “Amen” affirms that all creation has said is true and worthy; their silent prostration before “him who lives forever” is the only fit response to the vision. The scroll's worthy opener has been found, and the seals may now be broken.

witnesses.point: The golden bowls are the prayers of the saints — the prayers of God's lowliest people rise fragrant and accepted before the throne, not one of them lost.
The “new song” is consequent on redemption; the creatures who worshiped God now prostrate before the Lamb, giving him the same homage due God.
The redeemed are set nearer to God than the angels; they speak of themselves in the third person out of self-abasement, and the sevenfold applause answers the seven seals.
The final ascription is the chorus of the universe — God and the Lamb praised together on equal terms, a testimony to the Lamb's deity.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: That the same doxology in v.13 is offered jointly to the One on the throne and to the Lamb places the Lamb within the identity of the one God: to worship him is not to honor an exalted creature but to confess his full deity, and the “kingdom and priests” of v.10 fulfills the Sinai vocation of Exodus 19:6, now extended through the Lamb's blood to every nation.

8.application: Notice where the incense comes from: the prayers of the saints — the pressured, half-audible prayers of small churches under Rome — gathered into golden bowls and set before the throne of God. Your prayers are not lost in the noise of history; they rise as fragrance at the center of heaven's worship. And the song that answers them is not first about escape or vindication but about worth: “Worthy is the Lamb who was slain.” Worship is the church's truest weapon, and its object is a Lamb who bought you with his own blood.

_meta.title: Worthy Is the Lamb
reflection: The Lion John is promised turns out to be “a Lamb standing, as though slain” (v.6). Where are you still expecting God to conquer by force, when he means to conquer — in you and in the world — by the way of the cross?

The Lamb is worthy “for you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people... from every tribe and tongue and people and nation” (v.9). How does the price and the reach of that ransom reshape the way you regard believers unlike yourself?

The prayers of the saints rise as incense in golden bowls (v.8), and all creation ends by worshiping the One on the throne and the Lamb together (v.13). What would change if you treated worship, not anxiety, as your first response to a world that seems out of control?
